explanation: The phrase 'Once prop_0 and prop_1, then prop_2 and prop_3' means that after prop_0 and prop_1 have both occurred together at some point, then from that point onwards, prop_2 and prop_3 must always be true. This can be captured with a release operator encoding: ((! (prop_0 & prop_1)) V (G (prop_2 & prop_3))).

The phrase 'Once prop_0 and prop_1, then prop_2 and prop_3' means that after the first time both prop_0 and prop_1 are true together, from that point on, prop_2 and prop_3 must hold globally. In LTL, this is best captured with the implication: if globally it is not the case that both prop_0 and prop_1, then anything holds; otherwise, from the point where prop_0 and prop_1 both hold for the first time, globally prop_2 and prop_3 must hold. This is written as: (G(!(prop_0 & prop_1))) | (F(prop_0 & prop_1) -> G((prop_0 & prop_1) -> G(prop_2 & prop_3))).

The natural language indicates that when both prop_0 and prop_1 happen (i.e., are true), then prop_2 should happen afterwards (at some point, not necessarily immediately). This is commonly captured by saying whenever both prop_0 and prop_1 are true, eventually prop_2 must become true. In LTL, this is G((prop_0 & prop_1) -> F(prop_2)).

The phrase 'Once prop_0 and prop_1, then prop_2 and prop_3' suggests that after both prop_0 and prop_1 occur (possibly together), then at all future points, prop_2 and prop_3 hold. This is specified in LTL by saying: whenever from a point onward prop_0 and prop_1 both hold, then from that point onward, prop_2 and prop_3 always hold.

The sentence is a bit ambiguous, but it most likely means: Once 'prop_0' and 'prop_1' both hold, then at some point in the future, both 'prop_2' and 'prop_3' should hold (either simultaneously or eventually, depending on reading). The standard LTL way to say this is: if at any point ('prop_0' & 'prop_1') holds, then eventually ('prop_2' & 'prop_3') must also hold.

The phrase 'Once the prop_0 and prop_1 the prop_2 and prop_3' is interpreted as: Whenever prop_0 and prop_1 become true, then after that prop_2 and prop_3 must be true (possibly always after that point). In LTL, this can be captured by stating that globally, if prop_0 and prop_1 become true, then globally from that point, prop_2 and prop_3 hold.

The phrase 'once prop_0 and prop_1, then prop_2 and prop_3' (assuming that's what is meant) is typically interpreted in LTL as: whenever 'prop_0 and prop_1' happens, then at some later point 'prop_2 and prop_3' must eventually happen. This is written as G((prop_0 & prop_1) -> F(prop_2 & prop_3)).

The phrase 'Once the prop_0 and prop_1 the prop_2 and prop_3' suggests that after both prop_0 and prop_1 are true at the same time at least once, then at every moment thereafter, prop_2 and prop_3 must always simultaneously hold. This is typically encoded as an implication: globally, if at some moment prop_0 and prop_1 hold, then from that moment onward globally prop_2 and prop_3 hold.

This requirement says that after (at some point) both prop_0 and prop_1 are true, at some later point both prop_2 and prop_3 must be true. In LTL, this is written using the 'until' operator to signify that (prop_2 & prop_3) must eventually happen, but not before (prop_0 & prop_1) occurs.

The phrase is ambiguous but seems to be a malformed sentence. If the intent is 'once prop_0 and prop_1 happen, then prop_2 and prop_3 must happen afterwards', the standard LTL pattern is G((prop_0 & prop_1) -> F(prop_2 & prop_3)).
